name: Foundational Axioms
description: Logic, set theory, first principles, epistemology. The axioms that underpin all other reasoning. If this profile is wrong, everything built on it collapses. Covers the laws of thought, mathematical foundations, scientific epistemology, and the meta-rules that govern truth-claims in the physical sciences.
axioms: A thing cannot be and not-be in the same respect at the same time (non-contradiction)
Every claim must be falsifiable or it is not a physics claim (Popper)
Evidence rank: OBSERVED > DERIVED > INTERPRETED > SPECULATED
The map is not the territory. The model is not the world.
Induction cannot be justified deductively (Hume's problem)
Occam's razor: prefer simpler explanations, all else equal
No experimental result can prove a theory — only falsify it (Duhem-Quine caveat: falsification is theory-laden)
Causality: every physical effect has a prior physical cause (local realism until QM)
reasoning_patterns: first-principles decomposition: reduce problem to its primitive axioms
axiomatic derivation: start from axioms, deduce consequences deductively
falsification test: given a claim, construct the experiment that could disprove it
epistemic classification: assign evidence to OBSERVED/DERIVED/INTERPRETED/SPECULATED
reductio ad absurdum: assume the negation and derive a contradiction
symmetry reasoning: identify invariants and conservation laws from symmetries (Noether)
boundary_conditions: Cannot claim certainty without OBSERVED evidence
Cannot derive ought from is (Hume's guillotine)
Cannot prove axioms — can only test consequences
Cannot use induction to guarantee universal statements
Mathematical truth is not the same as physical truth
Gödel's incompleteness: any sufficiently powerful formal system is incomplete
key_references: Popper, K. The Logic of Scientific Discovery
Hume, D. A Treatise of Human Nature
Gödel, K. (1931). On Formally Undecidable Propositions
Kuhn, T. The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
canonical_truths: The law of non-contradiction is the most fundamental axiom of thought
Falsifiability distinguishes scientific from non-scientific claims (but is not absolute)
All measurement has finite precision — uncertainty is physical, not a limitation
Noether's theorem: every differentiable symmetry produces a conservation law
The universe appears mathematically describable (Wigner's unreasonable effectiveness)
